oneOf.description:
A `taggings` row must target exactly one of {object, unit}; `given` is how many
were set (§6.0b).
An alias's `scope` and `scope_ref` are inconsistent (§6.1a scope ↔ scope_ref).

scope.description: The scope an alias is valid in (§6.3b); paired with `scope_ref` (§6.1a consistency).

oneOf.description:
A `handles` row must set exactly one refinement of {unit, expression}; `given` is
how many were set (§6.3b/§6.1a).

`declared_by = ai` is structurally forbidden: AI proposals are review_items, never
canonical units; acceptance enters as `user` (§3.9/§6.0).

An op named a resolution/target kind whose machinery has not landed yet — e.g.
resolving an occurrence to `notation` before the notation registry exists (slice 2).
An op referenced a unit id that is not in the supplied content (stale read / bad id).
An op referenced an expression id that is not in the target unit's content.
An occurrence index is past the end of the expression's occurrence list.

`split_unit` was asked to split a unit whose content kind has no split semantics in
slice 1 (only `prose` is splittable).

`merge_units` was asked to merge units that are not mergeable (non-adjacent, different
parent/object, or a non-prose content kind).

A list of fresh ids did not match the count it had to cover (e.g. `new_tagging_ids`
vs the taggings to propagate). A glue minting bug, not a client error.

`materialize_object` was given an id remap that did not cover every {kind} in the
source content — copying with a partial map would alias ids across objects. Glue bug.
`split_unit` was asked to split past the end of the unit's prose text.

An inline atom (`math`/`reference`) carries a non-zero-width span — the prose-atom
contract requires `span.start == span.end` (its content lives in its own field, §6.0).
A glue/editor bug, not a client error.

An inline element's span falls outside its prose text — `start <= end <= text length`
(char offsets) is the §6.0 well-formedness rule. The ops maintain it by construction; the
import load path can't assume it, so it gates here (an out-of-bounds span mis-slices at the
render/editor boundary). A glue/import bug, not a client error.

An occurrence's selector span falls outside its expression's `surface_text` —
`start <= end <= surface_text length` (char offsets, §6.3a). Like inline spans, the ops
maintain it by construction (the surface serializer produces in-bounds selectors); the
import load path can't assume it, so it gates here — a corrupt selector is the slice-2
resolution substrate, so it must not reach storage. A glue/import bug, not a client error.

A content-derived edge (`from_content = true`) must record WHERE it came from —
both `source_unit_id` and `content_locator` (§6.1b).

`resolve_occurrence` was asked to resolve an occurrence that is already resolved —
re-resolving would overwrite the target and double-emit the edge (§6.3a).

`materialize_object`'s source content contains a duplicate {kind} id — copying would
alias the duplicates onto one fresh id via the remap. A glue/data bug.

OpOutcomeResult.title: OpOutcomeResult
OpOutcomeResult.description: Envelope of every unit-level canonical operation (all eight yield an `OpOutcome`).

Origin.title: Origin
Origin.description:
Provenance origin (arch doc §6.1). Stable, complete set; only `User` and `System`
are producible until the AI/import provenance columns land with their slices.

ParseStatus.title: ParseStatus
ParseStatus.description:
Whether a surface is usable, per OUR parser (arch doc §6.3a) — defined by `mathmeander`,
not LaTeX. Un-parseable input is never punished: it persists as `invalid` with
`original_input` intact (§2.2).

- `renderable`    — parses cleanly into a fully-formed AST.
- `partially_resolved` — parses, but with one or more recovered error fragments
  (`Expr::Error`); still renderable, with the bad parts shown verbatim.
- `invalid`       — nothing usable parsed (e.g. empty after trimming, or all error).
- `unresolved`    — reserved for "parsed, but has unresolved occurrences/references"
  (set by the core once occurrence resolution runs; the parser never emits it).

Provenance.title: Provenance
Provenance.description:
Provenance — the typed trust spine (arch doc §6.1). One row per provenanced fact;
origin-specific fields (model, prompt_template, context_snapshot_id, review_item_id,
source_id, source_locator) arrive as `Option` fields together with their target
tables — additive in Rust and SQL simultaneously.

oneOf.description: Prose + inline formatting + inline math + mentions.

items.description:
An inline element of prose (§6.0): minimal formatting, inline math, or a mention.

**The span contract** — `span` always char-indexes into the prose `text`, but two shapes:
  • An element that carries its **own content field** is a ZERO-WIDTH ATOM (`span = [p, p]`):
    its surface lives in that field, NOT in the prose `text`, so there is a single source of
    truth (§2.2 — no `Reference.text`-vs-`prose[span]` drift). `Math` (content in `expr`) and
    `Reference` (content in `text`) are atoms; the prose `text` holds no characters for them.
  • `Mark` carries no content — it is a REGION `[s, e)` overlaying real prose `text`
    (formatting). It may be non-empty.
Inline `$…$` math is thus a positional atom, not its own unit (else one sentence explodes into
units); promoting it to display math (`$$`) is `ops::toggle_expression_placement`. The
zero-width invariant for atoms is enforced by `validate::validate_inline` (not just convention),
so a future op/editor cannot silently reintroduce width. Rich formatting waits for the editor
(slice 2); MVP `Mark` carries an open-ended `style` tag so import/export round-trips losslessly.

oneOf.description: A formatting region over prose `text` — `span` may be non-empty (the marked range).

span.description:
A character-offset span into a surface (`[start, end)`), the wire span. Identical in
shape to the type the core embedded in slice 1a — relocated here (the surface owns the
char-span model) and re-exported by the core. The artifact `$defs` for it are byte-
identical to 1a's; the artifact HASH did move (the doc-comment-derived `description`
text changed), so do not hand-edit the recorded hash — it is regenerated by codegen.

oneOf.description: Inline math — a zero-width atom (`span = [p, p]`); content lives in `expr`.

span.description:
A character-offset span into a surface (`[start, end)`), the wire span. Identical in
shape to the type the core embedded in slice 1a — relocated here (the surface owns the
char-span model) and re-exported by the core. The artifact `$defs` for it are byte-
identical to 1a's; the artifact HASH did move (the doc-comment-derived `description`
text changed), so do not hand-edit the recorded hash — it is regenerated by codegen.

expr.description:
The one structure for all math, wherever it sits (§6.3a). Identity is STABLE and
presentation-independent; display mode is DERIVED from placement, so there is no mode
field. The `mathmeander` parser/serializer that fills these fields lands in slice 1b;
this is the wire shape it produces.

id.description:
Stable identity, minted in content (UUIDv7), unique workspace-wide — the
copy-mints-fresh / transclude-references rule hangs off this (§6.3a).

surface_text.description: The canonical surface in our grammar (names-canonical `mathmeander`).

original_input.description: Raw keystrokes, preserved verbatim — NEVER overwritten by normalization (§2.2).

parse_status.description:
Whether a surface is usable, per OUR parser (arch doc §6.3a) — defined by `mathmeander`,
not LaTeX. Un-parseable input is never punished: it persists as `invalid` with
`original_input` intact (§2.2).

- `renderable`    — parses cleanly into a fully-formed AST.
- `partially_resolved` — parses, but with one or more recovered error fragments
  (`Expr::Error`); still renderable, with the bad parts shown verbatim.
- `invalid`       — nothing usable parsed (e.g. empty after trimming, or all error).
- `unresolved`    — reserved for "parsed, but has unresolved occurrences/references"
  (set by the core once occurrence resolution runs; the parser never emits it).

occurrences.description:
Semantic refs inside the surface; each extracts to an edge (§6.1b). Always present
(`[]` when none) so the shape is explicit on both sides of the FFI.

items.description:
A semantic reference INSIDE a `MathExpression` (§6.3a). MVP selector is a coarse char
span (Q3); sub-term `Symbol` resolution (with `BoundVar`) is reserved. `target` is
absent while unresolved (the queue carries the unresolved text on the `links` row).

selector.description:
A character-offset span into a surface (`[start, end)`), the wire span. Identical in
shape to the type the core embedded in slice 1a — relocated here (the surface owns the
char-span model) and re-exported by the core. The artifact `$defs` for it are byte-
identical to 1a's; the artifact HASH did move (the doc-comment-derived `description`
text changed), so do not hand-edit the recorded hash — it is regenerated by codegen.

object_id.description: Identity of a canonical object (client-minted UUIDv7).

oneOf.description: Prose + inline formatting + inline math + mentions.

items.description:
An inline element of prose (§6.0): minimal formatting, inline math, or a mention.

**The span contract** — `span` always char-indexes into the prose `text`, but two shapes:
  • An element that carries its **own content field** is a ZERO-WIDTH ATOM (`span = [p, p]`):
    its surface lives in that field, NOT in the prose `text`, so there is a single source of
    truth (§2.2 — no `Reference.text`-vs-`prose[span]` drift). `Math` (content in `expr`) and
    `Reference` (content in `text`) are atoms; the prose `text` holds no characters for them.
  • `Mark` carries no content — it is a REGION `[s, e)` overlaying real prose `text`
    (formatting). It may be non-empty.
Inline `$…$` math is thus a positional atom, not its own unit (else one sentence explodes into
units); promoting it to display math (`$$`) is `ops::toggle_expression_placement`. The
zero-width invariant for atoms is enforced by `validate::validate_inline` (not just convention),
so a future op/editor cannot silently reintroduce width. Rich formatting waits for the editor
(slice 2); MVP `Mark` carries an open-ended `style` tag so import/export round-trips losslessly.

UnitContent.title: UnitContent
UnitContent.description:
A SINGLE unit's own authored material (§6.0) — never an embedded unit tree (children
are rows: `parent_unit_id` + `position` + `slot`). The internal `kind` tag is the
AUTHORITATIVE discriminator and the source of the derived `content_kind` SQL column.

UnitIdRemap.title: UnitIdRemap
UnitIdRemap.description: One old→new unit-id mapping (materialize re-homes copied units).

from.description:
Identity of a content unit (`content_units` row, §6.0b) — what annotations
anchor to, AI candidates target, and edges point at.

to.description:
Identity of a content unit (`content_units` row, §6.0b) — what annotations
anchor to, AI candidates target, and edges point at.

UnitLabel.title: UnitLabel
UnitLabel.description:
One unit's computed label facets (§6.3b, decision G). BOTH are returned; presentation
decides whether the name supersedes the number. `number`/`name` are independently optional.

unit_id.description:
Identity of a content unit (`content_units` row, §6.0b) — what annotations
anchor to, AI candidates target, and edges point at.

unit_type.description:
Echoed so presentation can render "Theorem 3" without re-reading the unit. `None` for
a plain (typeless) unit.

number.description:
The 1-based ordinal among its counter class; `None` when the policy doesn't number this
unit's type.

name.description:
A user name override (a `handle` on the unit, or — for an object `embed` — the embedded
object's `alias`). Where present it is what a chip shows; the `number` is still returned.

UnitStatus.title: UnitStatus
UnitStatus.description:
Per-unit crystallization state (§6.0/§2.4) — distinct from `MathExpression.parse_status`
(surface renderability) and from `ObjectStatus` (object lifecycle).

UnitType.title: UnitType
UnitType.description:
THE one user-facing math-flow label of a unit (§6.0). Carries *force and meaning*;
form lives separately in `UnitContent.kind`. Absent (`Option::None`) = plain content.
Forms (list/group/embed/derivation/case_split) are deliberately NOT here — they are
content kinds, never types (§6.0b).

oneOf.description: Client-minted ids must be UUIDv7 (arch doc §4/§6.3 — sortable, client-mintable).
First §6.1a origin-field invariant: `created_by` is required when origin = user.

origin.description:
Provenance origin (arch doc §6.1). Stable, complete set; only `User` and `System`
are producible until the AI/import provenance columns land with their slices.

oneOf.description: AI/import provenance is structurally impossible until its columns land (§6.1).

origin.description:
Provenance origin (arch doc §6.1). Stable, complete set; only `User` and `System`
are producible until the AI/import provenance columns land with their slices.

oneOf.description:
A stored value claims a schema_version newer than this core understands —
refusing loudly beats misreading user data (§2.2).

A `links` row must set exactly one target arm. Slice 1's arms are
`{target_object_id, unresolved_text}`; `given` is how many were set (§6.1a/§6.1b).

A deliberate edge (`from_content = false`) carries no object target — the typed
knowledge graph stays object-only, no off-graph deliberate edges (§6.1a).

`target_unit_id` is set without `target_object_id` — a unit refinement requires
its owning object target (§6.1a composite-FK discipline).

A typed graph edge resolved to a non-object target (notation/source/unresolved) —
every graph edge type requires an object target (§6.1a/§6.1b).

link_type.description:
Typed relationship of a `links` edge (§6.1b). Most are deliberate graph edges
(object-target, §6.1a); `related`/content-derived rows may stay unresolved.

oneOf.description:
A copied/derived object points back at its origin (§6.1b). Produced by the
`materialize_object` copy-and-edge stand-in (slice 1c); general enough to cover
future non-copy derivations (re-home vs origin-reference is slice 2, §18.5).

`target_selector` is set without `target_object_id` — a selector refines an object
target, never substitutes for one (§6.1a).

The derived `content_kind` would disagree with the unit's `content` tag (one fact,
one home — the generated column must equal the union tag, §6.0b).
A unit's `slot` is not valid for its parent's content kind (§6.0b/§6.1a).
`example_kind` is set on a unit whose `type` is not `example` (§6.0b).

A `*_detail.object_id` references an object of the wrong type (§6.1a
type-qualified references).

expected.description:
Durable workspace identity of an object (arch doc §6.0b: `object.type` ≠ `unit.type`).
Slice 1 widens this from the skeleton's lone `note` to the full §6 vocabulary: the
formal family (theorem/lemma/proposition/corollary/conjecture/claim), definition,
proof, example, question — all producible via create — plus source_excerpt, trail,
annotation, journal_day, which are **reserved vocabulary** (valid on read, not yet
producible — their detail tables / surfaces land in later slices, §6.1a/§13a).

given.description:
Durable workspace identity of an object (arch doc §6.0b: `object.type` ≠ `unit.type`).
Slice 1 widens this from the skeleton's lone `note` to the full §6 vocabulary: the
formal family (theorem/lemma/proposition/corollary/conjecture/claim), definition,
proof, example, question — all producible via create — plus source_excerpt, trail,
annotation, journal_day, which are **reserved vocabulary** (valid on read, not yet
producible — their detail tables / surfaces land in later slices, §6.1a/§13a).

oneOf.description:
The create path cannot produce this object type yet (reserved vocabulary whose
owning machinery lands in a later slice, §6.1a/§13a).

object_type.description:
Durable workspace identity of an object (arch doc §6.0b: `object.type` ≠ `unit.type`).
Slice 1 widens this from the skeleton's lone `note` to the full §6 vocabulary: the
formal family (theorem/lemma/proposition/corollary/conjecture/claim), definition,
proof, example, question — all producible via create — plus source_excerpt, trail,
annotation, journal_day, which are **reserved vocabulary** (valid on read, not yet
producible — their detail tables / surfaces land in later slices, §6.1a/§13a).

oneOf.description:
A valid, producible type that may not be minted by the DIRECT typed-create path — it
enters the graph by declaration → materialization (§9.y/§13a slice 2), never a raw POST.
Distinct from `TypeNotProducibleYet` (reserved, no machinery yet): here the machinery
exists (materialize / number / export); only the direct-create surface is gated.

object_type.description:
Durable workspace identity of an object (arch doc §6.0b: `object.type` ≠ `unit.type`).
Slice 1 widens this from the skeleton's lone `note` to the full §6 vocabulary: the
formal family (theorem/lemma/proposition/corollary/conjecture/claim), definition,
proof, example, question — all producible via create — plus source_excerpt, trail,
annotation, journal_day, which are **reserved vocabulary** (valid on read, not yet
producible — their detail tables / surfaces land in later slices, §6.1a/§13a).

oneOf.description:
A `taggings` row must target exactly one of {object, unit}; `given` is how many
were set (§6.0b).
An alias's `scope` and `scope_ref` are inconsistent (§6.1a scope ↔ scope_ref).

scope.description: The scope an alias is valid in (§6.3b); paired with `scope_ref` (§6.1a consistency).

oneOf.description:
A `handles` row must set exactly one refinement of {unit, expression}; `given` is
how many were set (§6.3b/§6.1a).

`declared_by = ai` is structurally forbidden: AI proposals are review_items, never
canonical units; acceptance enters as `user` (§3.9/§6.0).

An op named a resolution/target kind whose machinery has not landed yet — e.g.
resolving an occurrence to `notation` before the notation registry exists (slice 2).
An op referenced a unit id that is not in the supplied content (stale read / bad id).
An op referenced an expression id that is not in the target unit's content.
An occurrence index is past the end of the expression's occurrence list.

`split_unit` was asked to split a unit whose content kind has no split semantics in
slice 1 (only `prose` is splittable).

`merge_units` was asked to merge units that are not mergeable (non-adjacent, different
parent/object, or a non-prose content kind).

A list of fresh ids did not match the count it had to cover (e.g. `new_tagging_ids`
vs the taggings to propagate). A glue minting bug, not a client error.

`materialize_object` was given an id remap that did not cover every {kind} in the
source content — copying with a partial map would alias ids across objects. Glue bug.
`split_unit` was asked to split past the end of the unit's prose text.

An inline atom (`math`/`reference`) carries a non-zero-width span — the prose-atom
contract requires `span.start == span.end` (its content lives in its own field, §6.0).
A glue/editor bug, not a client error.

An inline element's span falls outside its prose text — `start <= end <= text length`
(char offsets) is the §6.0 well-formedness rule. The ops maintain it by construction; the
import load path can't assume it, so it gates here (an out-of-bounds span mis-slices at the
render/editor boundary). A glue/import bug, not a client error.

An occurrence's selector span falls outside its expression's `surface_text` —
`start <= end <= surface_text length` (char offsets, §6.3a). Like inline spans, the ops
maintain it by construction (the surface serializer produces in-bounds selectors); the
import load path can't assume it, so it gates here — a corrupt selector is the slice-2
resolution substrate, so it must not reach storage. A glue/import bug, not a client error.

A content-derived edge (`from_content = true`) must record WHERE it came from —
both `source_unit_id` and `content_locator` (§6.1b).

`resolve_occurrence` was asked to resolve an occurrence that is already resolved —
re-resolving would overwrite the target and double-emit the edge (§6.3a).

`materialize_object`'s source content contains a duplicate {kind} id — copying would
alias the duplicates onto one fresh id via the remap. A glue/data bug.

ValidationError.title: ValidationError
ValidationError.description:
A domain validation failure. Errors are VALUES crossing the FFI (result envelopes),
never exceptions (arch doc §17 boundary discipline).
